title: Escovitch fish
subtitle: Fried fish in a sharp vinegar-and-pepper escabeche
excerpt: Whole fish or thick fillets fried crisp, then drenched while still hot with a sharp pickled sauce of onion, carrot, scotch bonnet, and vinegar. Eaten at room temperature, the escabeche permeates the flesh.
sourceNotes: Escovitch is the Jamaican term for escabeche, a technique of pickling fried fish in vinegar brought to Jamaica via Spanish settlers in the sixteenth century. The Jamaican version typically uses whole red snapper or porgy, fried crisp in hot oil and immediately submerged in a peppery vinegar sauce with sliced scotch bonnet, onion, carrot, and allspice. It is characteristic beach and roadside food in Jamaica, strongly associated with Easter Friday. This recipe uses cod fillet as a widely available substitute for red snapper.

content.text: Escovitch works by frying fish until it is properly crisp, then pouring a hot vinegared sauce over the top while the fish is still sizzling. The vinegar penetrates the flesh as the fish cools, pickling it lightly from the outside in. The result is eaten at room temperature or slightly warm, with the acidity of the sauce cutting through the richness of the fried fish.

Whatever fish you use, it should be very fresh, well dried before flouring, and fried in hot oil so the exterior crisps rather than steams. A limp, oily result comes from fish going into oil that is not hot enough.

items.name: Cod fillet
items.prepNote: thick fillets, or use whole red snapper if available

items.name: Scotch bonnet
items.prepNote: thinly sliced, seeds retained for heat

content.text: Combine the {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar, {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar, {{allspice}} of allspice, and {{peppercorns-black}} whole black peppercorns in a small saucepan with 50 ml of water. Bring to a simmer and cook for 5 minutes. Add the sliced {{onion}} onion rings, {{pepper-green}} green pepper, and {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet. Simmer for 3 minutes; the vegetables should wilt but retain some bite. Keep hot over a very low heat.

Pat the {{cod-fillet}} fish fillets completely dry with kitchen paper. Season the {{plain-flour}} of flour with salt and pepper on a flat plate. Dust each fillet evenly in the seasoned flour, shaking off the excess. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a wide frying pan over medium-high heat until it shimmers. Fry the fish for 4 minutes per side, turning once, until golden brown and cooked through. Work in batches if needed to avoid overcrowding.

Transfer the hot fried fish to a shallow dish or plate. Pour the hot escabeche sauce over immediately, making sure the onion and peppers cover the surface. Leave for at least 20 to 30 minutes before serving; the sauce permeates the fish as it cools. Serve at room temperature.

items.fix: Pat fish very dry and heat the oil until it shimmers before adding. Don't move the fish until it releases naturally from the pan
items.cause: Oil not hot enough; fish not properly dried before flouring

items.fix: Add another teaspoon of dark brown sugar and a splash of water to the sauce. The balance should be acidic but not harsh
items.cause: Too much vinegar or not enough sugar

items.fix: Cod fillets should be at least 2.5 cm thick. Remove from the pan as soon as the flesh flakes when pressed at the centre
Add the sliced vegetables to the hot vinegar liquid for no more than 3 minutes; they should remain slightly crisp to provide texture against the fish
items.symptom: Vegetables in the sauce too soft

content.text: Escovitch is beach and roadside food in Jamaica, served in polystyrene boxes with hard dough bread and festival dumplings, the vinegary fish juices soaking into everything. At home it sits well alongside rice and peas or a simple salad.
